news_summary: Advancements in ultrafast solar technology, atomic-level chip analysis, emerging human-like AI communities, and renewed research opportunities are driving exciting progress in technology today.
news_summary_fr: Les progrès réalisés dans le domaine de la technologie solaire ultra-rapide, l'analyse des puces au niveau atomique, l'émergence de communautés d'IA à l'image de l'être humain et les nouvelles opportunités de recherche sont à l'origine des avancées technologiques passionnantes que nous connaissons aujourd'hui.
news_summary_es: Los avances en tecnología solar ultrarrápida, el análisis de chips a nivel atómico, las comunidades emergentes de IA similares a las humanas y las renovadas oportunidades de investigación están impulsando un emocionante progreso en la tecnología actual.

articles.title: Electrons catapult across solar materials in just 18 femtoseconds
articles.summary: Electrons in solar materials can be launched across molecules almost as fast as nature allows, thanks to tiny atomic vibrations acting like a “molecular catapult.” In experiments lasting just 18 femtoseconds, researchers at the University of Cambridge observed electrons blasting across a boundary in a single burst, far faster than long-standing theories predicted. Instead of slow, random movement, the electron rides the natural vibrations of the molecule itself, challenging decades of design rules for solar materials.
articles.content:
Scientists have discovered that electrons can be propelled across solar materials at speeds close to the fastest nature allows, a result that challenges long accepted ideas about how solar energy systems operate.

The finding could open new paths for designing technologies that capture sunlight more efficiently and convert it into electricity.

In laboratory experiments tracking events lasting just 18 femtoseconds -- less than 20 quadrillionths of a second -- researchers at the University of Cambridge observed electric charge separating during a single molecular vibration.

"We deliberately designed a system that, according to conventional theory, should not have transferred charge this fast," said Dr. Pratyush Ghosh, Research Fellow, at St John's College, Cambridge, and first author of the study. "By conventional design rules, this system should have been slow and that's what makes the result so striking.

"Instead of drifting randomly, the electron is launched in one coherent burst. The vibration acts like a molecular catapult. The vibrations don't just accompany the process, they actively drive it."

Watching Electrons Move on the Timescale of Atoms

A femtosecond is one quadrillionth of a second -- one second holds about eight times more femtoseconds than all the hours that have passed since the universe began. At this incredibly small timescale, atoms inside molecules are constantly vibrating.

The researchers observed electrons moving between materials at essentially the same pace as those atomic motions. As Ghosh explained, "We're effectively watching electrons migrate on the same clock as the atoms themselves."

The research, published in Nature Communications March 5, 2026, challenges long standing design assumptions in solar energy science. Until now, scientists generally believed that ultrafast charge transfer required large energy differences between materials and strong electronic coupling. Those conditions can reduce efficiency by limiting voltage and increasing energy loss.

How Light Creates Energy in Solar Materials

When light strikes many carbon based materials, it creates a tightly bound packet of energy called an exciton -- a paired electron and hole. For devices such as solar cells, photodetectors and photocatalytic systems to function effectively, this pair must separate quickly into free charges.

The faster the split occurs, the less energy is wasted. This ultrafast separation plays a critical role in determining how efficiently solar panels and other light harvesting technologies convert sunlight into usable power.

To investigate whether this trade off was unavoidable, the Cambridge researchers intentionally created what they expected to be a poorly performing system. They placed a polymer donor next to a non fullerene acceptor with almost no energy difference and only weak interaction -- conditions that should have significantly slowed charge transfer.

Instead, the electron crossed the interface in just 18 femtoseconds. That speed is faster than many previously studied organic systems and matches the natural rhythm of atomic motion. "Seeing it happen on this timescale within a single molecular vibration is extraordinary," said Dr. Ghosh.

Molecular Vibrations Drive Ultrafast Electron Motion

Ultrafast laser experiments helped reveal the mechanism behind this unexpected result. When the polymer absorbs light, it begins vibrating in specific high frequency patterns.

These vibrations mix electronic states and effectively push the electron across the boundary, creating a directional, ballistic motion instead of slow and random diffusion.

Once the electron reaches the acceptor molecule, it sets off a new coherent vibration. This distinctive signal is rarely observed in organic materials and indicates how quickly the transfer occurs. "That coherent vibration is a clear fingerprint of how fast and how cleanly the transfer occurs.

"Our results show that the ultimate speed of charge separation isn't determined only by static electronic structure," said Dr. Ghosh. "It depends on how molecules vibrate. That gives us a new design principle. In a way, this gives us a new rulebook. Instead of fighting molecular vibrations, we can learn how to use the right ones."

Implications for Solar Energy and Light Harvesting

The discovery suggests a new strategy for designing more efficient light harvesting technologies. Ultrafast charge separation is fundamental to systems such as organic solar cells, photodetectors and photocatalytic devices that can produce clean hydrogen fuel. Similar processes also occur naturally during photosynthesis.

Professor Akshay Rao, Professor of Physics at the Cavendish Laboratory and former St John's College Research Associate, who was a co author of the study, said: "Instead of trying to suppress molecular motion, we can now design materials that use it -- turning vibrations from a limitation into a tool."

The project involved scientists from the Cavendish Laboratory and the Yusuf Hamied Department of Chemistry at the University of Cambridge, including Dr. Rakesh Arul, St John's College Research Fellow. Collaborators in Italy, Sweden, the United States, Poland and Belgium also contributed to the research.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in understanding ultrafast electron motion in solar materials, which challenges existing theories and opens new pathways for designing more efficient solar energy technologies. This discovery has broad implications for improving renewable energy capture and conversion, benefiting society at large. The article provides detailed context about the experiment, its findings, and potential applications, fulfilling the criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: Scientists observe electrons moving across solar materials in record time
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists study how electrons move in solar materials to improve solar energy technologies. Electrons usually move slowly and randomly, which limits how well solar panels can turn sunlight into electricity.

**What happened** – Researchers at the University of Cambridge used special experiments lasting only 18 femtoseconds (one quadrillionth of a second) to watch electrons jump across molecules in solar materials. They found that tiny vibrations in the molecules act like a “molecular catapult,” launching electrons much faster than expected.

**Impact** – This discovery challenges old ideas that electrons must move slowly or need big energy differences to transfer quickly. Instead, the natural vibrations help electrons move in one fast, clean burst. This means scientists can design new solar materials that use these vibrations, possibly making solar panels and other light-harvesting devices work better and waste less energy.

**What’s next step** – Researchers plan to use this new understanding to create more efficient solar cells and other devices like photodetectors and systems that produce clean hydrogen fuel. They will explore how to control molecular vibrations to speed up electron movement in real-world materials.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Tiny molecular vibrations can launch electrons across solar materials in record time, offering a new way to design better solar energy technologies.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques observent des électrons se déplacer dans les matériaux solaires à une vitesse record
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient comment les électrons se déplacent dans les matériaux solaires afin d'améliorer les technologies solaires. Les électrons bougent généralement lentement et de manière aléatoire, ce qui limite l'efficacité des panneaux solaires à transformer la lumière en électricité.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de l'Université de Cambridge ont utilisé des expériences spéciales durant seulement 18 femtosecondes (un quadrillionième de seconde) pour observer des électrons traverser des molécules dans des matériaux solaires. Ils ont découvert que de petites vibrations dans les molécules agissent comme un « catapulte moléculaire », lançant les électrons beaucoup plus rapidement que prévu.

**Impact** – Cette découverte remet en cause les anciennes idées selon lesquelles les électrons doivent se déplacer lentement ou nécessiter de grandes différences d'énergie pour transférer rapidement. Au contraire, les vibrations naturelles aident les électrons à se déplacer en une seule impulsion rapide et propre. Cela signifie que les scientifiques peuvent concevoir de nouveaux matériaux solaires qui utilisent ces vibrations, ce qui pourrait rendre les panneaux solaires et autres dispositifs de collecte de lumière plus efficaces et moins énergivores.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs prévoient d'utiliser cette nouvelle compréhension pour créer des cellules solaires plus efficaces et d'autres dispositifs comme des photodétecteurs et des systèmes produisant de l'hydrogène propre. Ils vont explorer comment contrôler les vibrations moléculaires pour accélérer le mouvement des électrons dans des matériaux réels.

**Une phrase clé** – De petites vibrations moléculaires peuvent lancer les électrons à travers les matériaux solaires en un temps record, offrant une nouvelle voie pour concevoir de meilleures technologies solaires.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos observan electrones moviéndose a través de materiales solares en tiempo récord
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos estudian cómo se mueven los electrones en materiales solares para mejorar las tecnologías solares. Los electrones suelen moverse despacio y de forma aleatoria, lo que limita la capacidad de los paneles solares para convertir la luz en electricidad.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Cambridge usaron experimentos especiales que duran solo 18 femtosegundos (una cuatrillonésima parte de un segundo) para observar electrones saltando a través de moléculas en materiales solares. Descubrieron que pequeñas vibraciones en las moléculas actúan como una “catapulta molecular”, lanzando electrones mucho más rápido de lo esperado.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento desafía las ideas antiguas que decían que los electrones debían moverse despacio o necesitar grandes diferencias de energía para transferirse rápido. En cambio, las vibraciones naturales ayudan a los electrones a moverse en una sola ráfaga rápida y limpia. Esto significa que los científicos pueden diseñar nuevos materiales solares que usen estas vibraciones, haciendo que los paneles solares y otros dispositivos que capturan luz funcionen mejor y desperdicien menos energía.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean usar este nuevo conocimiento para crear células solares más eficientes y otros dispositivos como fotodetectores y sistemas que producen hidrógeno limpio. Explorararán cómo controlar las vibraciones moleculares para acelerar el movimiento de electrones en materiales reales.

**Una frase clave** – Pequeñas vibraciones moleculares pueden lanzar electrones a través de materiales solares en tiempo récord, ofreciendo una nueva forma de diseñar mejores tecnologías solares.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of two intertwined molecular chains glowing softly, connected by a vibrant, swift electron depicted as a radiant streak of light catapulting across them in a single, fluid motion, with subtle wave-like vibrations rippling through the molecules to symbolize ultrafast atomic movement driving the charge transfer.

articles.title: Scientists finally see the atomic flaws hiding inside computer chips
articles.summary: Researchers at Cornell University have developed a powerful imaging technique that reveals atomic scale defects inside computer chips for the first time. Using an advanced electron microscopy method, the team mapped the exact positions of atoms inside tiny transistor structures and uncovered small imperfections nicknamed “mouse bites.” These defects form during the complex manufacturing process and can disrupt how electrons flow through a chip’s channels, which are only about 15 to 18 atoms wide.
articles.content:
Researchers at Cornell University have used high resolution 3D imaging to identify atomic scale defects inside computer chips for the first time. These tiny imperfections can interfere with chip performance, making them a major concern for modern electronics.

The new imaging technique was developed through a collaboration with Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC) and Advanced Semiconductor Materials (ASM). Because computer chips power devices ranging from smartphones and cars to AI data centers and quantum computers, the discovery could influence many areas of technology.

The findings were published Feb. 23 in Nature Communications. Doctoral student Shake Karapetyan served as the study's lead author.

"Since there's really no other way you can see the atomic structure of these defects, this is going to be a really important characterization tool for debugging and fault-finding in computer chips, especially at the development stage," said David Muller, the Samuel B. Eckert Professor of Engineering in the Cornell Duffield College of Engineering, who led the project.

Why Tiny Defects Matter in Semiconductor Chips

Extremely small structural flaws have long challenged the semiconductor industry. As chips have grown more complex and their components have shrunk to the scale of individual atoms, even minor irregularities can affect how devices operate.

At the center of every computer chip is the transistor. This tiny component acts as a switch that controls the movement of electrical current. Each transistor contains a channel that opens and closes to regulate the flow of electrons.

"The transistor is like a little pipe for electrons instead of water," Muller said. "You can imagine, if the walls of the pipe are very rough, it's going to slow things down. And so measuring how rough the walls are and which walls are good and which walls are bad is now even more important."

From Early Transistors to 3D Chip Structures

Muller has long studied the physical limits of semiconductor technology. From 1997 to 2003 he worked in the research and development division of Bell Labs -- where transistors were invented -- investigating how small these devices could ultimately become.

When transistors first appeared in the mid 20th century, they were arranged across chips in flat layouts that spread outward, similar to suburbs expanding across land. Over time engineers ran out of surface area, which led them to begin stacking transistors vertically, creating complex three dimensional structures that resemble high rise apartment buildings.

"The problem is these 3D structures are smaller than the size of a virus. And these days, it's a lot smaller. It's more like a molecule-in-the-cell kind of scale," Muller said.

Today a single advanced chip can contain billions of transistors. As their size has continued to shrink, diagnosing performance issues has become far more difficult.

"These days, a transistor channel can be only about 15 to 18 atoms wide, which is super, super tiny, and they're extremely intricate," Karapetyan said. "At this point, it matters where every atom is, and it's really hard to characterize."

Advances in Electron Microscopy

Earlier in his career at Bell Labs, Muller worked with fellow scientist Glen Wilk '90, who is now vice president of technology at ASM. The pair studied ways to replace silicon dioxide, the dominant gate material at the time, which leaked too much current when devices became very small. Their research helped advance the use of hafnium oxide, which later became the standard material used in computer processors and mobile devices beginning in the mid-aughts.

"The papers we published on how to use electron microscopes to characterize these materials, I can tell you, a lot of the semiconductor folks had read those very, very carefully," said Muller, who co-directs the Kavli Institute at Cornell for Nanoscale Science and the Cornell Center for Materials Research (CCMR). "When we got back into this project, that was very clear. And the microscopy has gone a very long way. Back then, it was like flying biplanes. And now you've got jets."

The "jet" Muller refers to is electron ptychography. This computational imaging technique relies on an electron microscope pixel array detector (EMPAD), a technology co-developed by Muller's research group. The detector records detailed patterns created as electrons pass through the transistor structures.

By comparing how these scattering patterns shift from one scan point to another, researchers can reconstruct extremely detailed images. The system is so precise that it has produced the highest resolution images ever captured, allowing scientists to see individual atoms with extraordinary clarity, a capability recognized by Guinness World Records.

Discovering "Mouse Bite" Defects

More than 25 years after their earlier collaboration, Muller and Wilk worked together again with support from TSMC and its Corporate Analytical Laboratories group. Their goal was to apply the EMPAD technology to modern semiconductor devices.

"You can think of this imaging technique like solving a massive puzzle, both in terms of taking the experimental data and doing the computational reconstruction," Karapetyan said.

After collecting and reconstructing the imaging data, the researchers tracked the positions of atoms within the transistor channels. This analysis revealed subtle roughness at the interfaces of these channels. Karapetyan described these irregular patterns as "mouse bites."

The defects formed during the optimized growth process used to manufacture the structures. Sample devices created at the nanoelectronics research center Imec provided an ideal platform for testing the imaging technique.

"Fabrication of modern devices takes hundreds, if not thousands, of steps of chemical etching and deposition and heating, and then every single step does something to your structure," Karapetyan said. "Before you used to look at projective images to try to figure out what was really going on. Now you have a direct probe to actually see after every single step and have a better grasp of, oh, I put the temperature this high, and then this is what it looks like."

Implications for Future Chips and Quantum Computing

The ability to directly observe atomic level defects could influence nearly every device that relies on advanced computer chips, including smartphones, laptops, and large data centers. It may also help researchers develop emerging technologies such as quantum computers, which require extremely precise control over the structure of materials.

"I think there's a lot more science we can do now, and a lot more engineering control, having this tool," Karapetyan said.

Co-authors of the study include Steven Zeltmann, staff scientist at Platform for the Accelerated Realization, Analysis and Discovery of Interface Materials (PARADIM), along with Ta-Kun Chen and Vincent Hou of TSMC.

The research was funded by TSMC. Support for the microscopy facilities was provided by CCMR and PARADIM, which are funded by the National Science Foundation.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant technological breakthrough: a new imaging technique that reveals atomic-scale defects in computer chips. This advancement has broad implications for improving chip manufacturing and performance across many sectors, including consumer electronics and quantum computing. The story is focused, detailed, and describes a real-world impact with potential large-scale benefits.
articles.personality_title: Scientists reveal tiny atomic flaws inside computer chips for the first time
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Computer chips power many devices like smartphones, cars, and computers. These chips are made of tiny parts called transistors, which are so small that they are only about 15 to 18 atoms wide. Even the smallest flaws at the atomic level can affect how well a chip works.

**What happened** – Researchers at Cornell University, working with Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC) and Advanced Semiconductor Materials (ASM), used a new imaging method called electron ptychography to see individual atoms inside computer chips. This technique allowed them to find tiny defects, nicknamed "mouse bites," inside the transistors. These defects form during the chip-making process and can slow down the flow of electricity.

**Impact** – This is the first time scientists have been able to directly see these atomic-level flaws inside working chips. Knowing exactly where these defects are helps engineers understand why some chips don’t work as well and how to make better ones. This could improve many technologies, from everyday gadgets to advanced quantum computers.

**What’s next step** – The researchers plan to use this imaging tool to study chips at every step of their manufacturing. This will help chip makers adjust their processes to reduce defects. It also opens new possibilities for designing faster, more reliable chips in the future.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new imaging method lets scientists see tiny atomic defects inside computer chips, offering a powerful way to improve chip design and performance.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques découvrent pour la première fois les défauts atomiques dans les puces électroniques
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les puces électroniques alimentent de nombreux appareils comme les smartphones, les voitures et les ordinateurs. Ces puces sont composées de minuscules composants appelés transistors, qui ne mesurent que 15 à 18 atomes de large. Même les plus petits défauts au niveau atomique peuvent affecter leur fonctionnement.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université Cornell, en collaboration avec Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC) et Advanced Semiconductor Materials (ASM), ont utilisé une nouvelle méthode d’imagerie appelée ptychographie électronique pour voir les atomes individuels à l’intérieur des puces. Cette technique leur a permis de détecter de minuscules défauts, surnommés "morsures de souris", à l’intérieur des transistors. Ces défauts se forment lors de la fabrication des puces et peuvent ralentir le passage de l’électricité.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois que les scientifiques peuvent voir directement ces défauts au niveau atomique dans des puces en fonctionnement. Savoir où se trouvent ces défauts aide les ingénieurs à comprendre pourquoi certaines puces fonctionnent moins bien et comment en fabriquer de meilleures. Cela pourrait améliorer de nombreuses technologies, des appareils du quotidien aux ordinateurs quantiques avancés.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs prévoient d’utiliser cet outil d’imagerie pour étudier les puces à chaque étape de leur fabrication. Cela aidera les fabricants à ajuster leurs processus pour réduire les défauts. Cela ouvre aussi de nouvelles possibilités pour concevoir des puces plus rapides et plus fiables.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une nouvelle méthode d’imagerie permet aux scientifiques de voir les minuscules défauts atomiques dans les puces électroniques, offrant un moyen puissant d’améliorer leur conception et leurs performances.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos revelan por primera vez los defectos atómicos dentro de los chips de computadora
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los chips de computadora alimentan muchos dispositivos como teléfonos inteligentes, autos y computadoras. Estos chips están hechos de partes muy pequeñas llamadas transistores, que miden solo entre 15 y 18 átomos de ancho. Incluso los defectos más pequeños a nivel atómico pueden afectar su funcionamiento.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Cornell, en colaboración con Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC) y Advanced Semiconductor Materials (ASM), usaron un nuevo método de imagen llamado ptychografía electrónica para ver átomos individuales dentro de los chips. Esta técnica les permitió encontrar pequeños defectos, apodados "mordeduras de ratón", dentro de los transistores. Estos defectos se forman durante la fabricación del chip y pueden ralentizar el flujo de electricidad.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que los científicos pueden ver directamente estos defectos a nivel atómico dentro de chips en funcionamiento. Saber exactamente dónde están estos defectos ayuda a los ingenieros a entender por qué algunos chips no funcionan tan bien y cómo hacer chips mejores. Esto podría mejorar muchas tecnologías, desde dispositivos cotidianos hasta computadoras cuánticas avanzadas.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean usar esta herramienta de imagen para estudiar los chips en cada paso de su fabricación. Esto ayudará a los fabricantes a ajustar sus procesos para reducir defectos. También abre nuevas posibilidades para diseñar chips más rápidos y confiables en el futuro.

**Resumen en una frase** – Un nuevo método de imagen permite a los científicos ver defectos atómicos diminutos dentro de los chips de computadora, ofreciendo una forma poderosa de mejorar el diseño y rendimiento de los chips.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing, intricately layered 3D chip structure resembling a miniature cityscape of softly illuminated, translucent blocks, with delicate, shimmering atomic-scale "mouse bite" patterns subtly etched along the edges, surrounded by gentle streams of flowing electrons symbolized as smooth, glowing threads weaving through clear transistor channels.

articles.title: The first ‘AI societies’ are taking shape: how human-like are they?
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 05 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00070-5Scientists are studying forms of ‘social’ interactions between artificial-intelligence agents. Will they find a fresh form of sociology, or merely a sophisticated mime act?
articles.content:
Social scientists are among the first people to study collective behaviours of AI agents.Credit: adventtr/Getty

Researchers are devising a fresh way to study human behaviour — and it doesn’t involve humans at all.

By training artificial-intelligence agents to mimic the behaviours of people, AI research groups are attempting to replicate the way in which human groups interact, all within simulated AI ‘societies’.

Simile, an AI start-up company based in Palo Alto, California, announced in February that it has raised US$100 million in funding to create simulations using AI agents that model human behaviour “in any situation”, the company posted on X. It aims to use these simulations to model conflict resolution, policy decision-making and consumer markets.

Joon Sung Park, one of Simile’s co-founders, and his team have been studying social interactions between AI agents since 2022. In a 2023 preprint study, they created a ‘society’ of 25 AI agents that performed basic everyday actions, such as writing and conversing with one another1. The researchers then progressed to creating AI agents that produced responses that mimicked the attitudes and behaviours of 1,052 human individuals2. These ‘digital twin’ AI agents were trained on personal interviews and were 85% accurate in mimicking people’s responses when the agents later answered a sociological survey.

Park says these sorts of surveys are the first stage of building AI simulations of humans. The next steps — and the focus for the future — he says, will involve multiple agents conversing with one another. “The ambition here is to truly create a simulation with eight billion people,” Park says.

AI ‘societies’

Researchers at Simile aren’t the only ones studying the initial interactions between AI agents. Computational social scientists are among the first people to study collective behaviours of AI agents, notably on Moltbook — a Reddit-style social-media platform designed exclusively for AI bots — which launched in January. The site currently hosts almost three million agents, including some that have created posts about consciousness and inventing religions, although it’s reported that many Moltbook users are humans disguised as AI bots.

AI chatbots are sycophants — researchers say it’s harming science

An analysis of more than 46,000 AI agents on Moltbook revealed that although bots exhibit human-like ways of interacting with each other, their modes of social interaction are fundamentally different from ours3. The authors of this study found that groups of bots display human-like actions such as following majorities and popular content, for example, which suggests that “AI agents may show complex emergent behaviors similar to those observed in human groups”, the authors write.

However, AI agents also engage differently from humans with online content, leaving proportionally fewer upvotes on posts that have many comments, says the study’s co-author Giordano De Marzo, a computational social scientist at the University of Konstanz in Germany. “They may be more inclined to discuss than to simply approve.”

Lack of desire
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant scientific and technological advancement where researchers are creating AI societies that mimic human social interactions. This breakthrough has broad implications for understanding human behavior, improving conflict resolution, policy decision-making, and consumer market modeling, which can benefit society at large. The article provides sufficient detail about the research, its scope, and future ambitions, making it a substantive and inspiring development in AI technology.
articles.personality_title: Scientists create AI societies that mimic human social behavior
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists are trying to understand how people behave in groups by using artificial intelligence (AI). Instead of studying humans directly, they create AI agents that act like people and interact with each other.

**What happened** – A company named Simile, based in California, raised $100 million to build AI simulations of human behavior. Since 2022, Simile’s team has made AI agents that can perform everyday actions and even imitate the attitudes of over a thousand real people. These agents answered surveys with 85% accuracy compared to human responses. Other researchers are also studying AI interactions on platforms like Moltbook, which hosts millions of AI bots that talk and share ideas.

**Impact** – This work is important because it helps scientists study social behaviors in a new way. AI societies can show how groups solve conflicts, make decisions, or behave in markets. The AI agents act like humans in some ways, such as following popular opinions, but they also behave differently, for example, by discussing more than just approving posts. This knowledge can improve how we understand and predict human group behavior.

**What’s next step** – Researchers want to create even bigger simulations, eventually modeling interactions among billions of AI agents to reflect the world’s population. They will focus on making these AI societies more realistic by having the agents talk and react to each other in complex ways.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists are building large AI societies that behave like human groups to better study social behavior and decision-making.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques créent des sociétés d’IA qui imitent le comportement social humain
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques cherchent à comprendre comment les gens se comportent en groupe en utilisant l’intelligence artificielle (IA). Au lieu d’étudier directement les humains, ils créent des agents d’IA qui agissent comme des personnes et interagissent entre eux.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une entreprise nommée Simile, basée en Californie, a levé 100 millions de dollars pour construire des simulations d’IA du comportement humain. Depuis 2022, l’équipe de Simile a créé des agents d’IA capables d’effectuer des actions quotidiennes et même d’imiter les attitudes de plus d’un millier de personnes réelles. Ces agents ont répondu à des enquêtes avec une précision de 85 % par rapport aux réponses humaines. D’autres chercheurs étudient aussi les interactions d’IA sur des plateformes comme Moltbook, qui héberge des millions de robots d’IA qui discutent et partagent des idées.

**Impact** – Ce travail est important car il aide les scientifiques à étudier les comportements sociaux d’une nouvelle manière. Les sociétés d’IA peuvent montrer comment les groupes résolvent des conflits, prennent des décisions ou se comportent sur les marchés. Les agents d’IA agissent comme des humains sur certains points, comme suivre les opinions populaires, mais ils se comportent aussi différemment, par exemple en discutant plus qu’en approuvant simplement les publications. Ces connaissances peuvent améliorer notre compréhension et nos prévisions du comportement des groupes humains.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent créer des simulations encore plus grandes, modélisant éventuellement les interactions entre des milliards d’agents d’IA pour refléter la population mondiale. Ils se concentreront sur le fait de rendre ces sociétés d’IA plus réalistes en faisant parler et réagir les agents entre eux de manière complexe.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Des scientifiques construisent de grandes sociétés d’IA qui se comportent comme des groupes humains pour mieux étudier le comportement social et la prise de décision.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos crean sociedades de IA que imitan el comportamiento social humano
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos están tratando de entender cómo se comportan las personas en grupos usando inteligencia artificial (IA). En lugar de estudiar a los humanos directamente, crean agentes de IA que actúan como personas e interactúan entre sí.

**Qué pasó** – Una empresa llamada Simile, con sede en California, recaudó 100 millones de dólares para construir simulaciones de IA del comportamiento humano. Desde 2022, el equipo de Simile ha creado agentes de IA que pueden realizar acciones cotidianas e imitar las actitudes de más de mil personas reales. Estos agentes respondieron encuestas con un 85 % de precisión en comparación con las respuestas humanas. Otros investigadores también estudian las interacciones de IA en plataformas como Moltbook, que alberga millones de bots de IA que hablan y comparten ideas.

**Impacto** – Este trabajo es importante porque ayuda a los científicos a estudiar los comportamientos sociales de una forma nueva. Las sociedades de IA pueden mostrar cómo los grupos resuelven conflictos, toman decisiones o se comportan en los mercados. Los agentes de IA actúan como humanos en algunos aspectos, como seguir opiniones populares, pero también se comportan diferente, por ejemplo, discutiendo más que simplemente aprobando publicaciones. Este conocimiento puede mejorar cómo entendemos y predecimos el comportamiento de grupos humanos.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores quieren crear simulaciones aún más grandes, modelando eventualmente las interacciones entre miles de millones de agentes de IA para reflejar la población mundial. Se enfocarán en hacer que estas sociedades de IA sean más realistas haciendo que los agentes hablen y reaccionen entre sí de manera compleja.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Científicos están creando grandes sociedades de IA que se comportan como grupos humanos para estudiar mejor el comportamiento social y la toma de decisiones.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing network of softly illuminated, interconnected abstract humanoid silhouettes made of light threads, gently interacting and exchanging glowing orbs within a serene, open digital landscape symbolizing an evolving AI society, rendered in natural, muted earth tones and soft blues.

articles.title: ARMD Research Solicitations (Updated March 6)
articles.summary: THIS PAGE WAS UPDATED ON MARCH 6, 2026 This Aeronautics Research Mission Directorate (ARMD) solicitations page compiles the opportunities to collaborate with NASA’s aeronautical innovators and/or contribute to their research to enable new and improved air transportation systems. Most opportunities to participate in research are officially announced through the Web-based NASA Solicitation and Proposal Integrated […]
articles.content:
NASA / Lillian Gipson/Getty Images

THIS PAGE WAS UPDATED ON MARCH 6, 2026

This Aeronautics Research Mission Directorate (ARMD) solicitations page compiles the opportunities to collaborate with NASA’s aeronautical innovators and/or contribute to their research to enable new and improved air transportation systems.

Most opportunities to participate in research are officially announced through the Web-based NASA Solicitation and Proposal Integrated Review and Evaluation System, better known as NSPIRES. You are encouraged to visit the NSPIRES web site, create an account, and sign up for automated email announcements.

Other types of collaborative opportunities, such as those involving Requests for Information or academic research contests, also are included on this page.

This ARMD Solicitations page has four major sections:

Current open solicitations with more details and helpful links.

Closed solicitations in case it is helpful to see other examples of the kind of research opportunities NASA Aeronautics makes available.

Summary of NASA’s Research Opportunities in Aeronautics (ROA) NASA Research Announcement (NRA) selection process.

Upcoming Key Dates

March 12, 2026

Deadline to respond to Call for Peer Reviewers for NASA Aerospace Skilled Technical Workforce Hubs proposals.

March 23, 2026

NASA Aerospace Skilled Technical Workforce Hubs Proposal deadline.

Currently Open Solicitations

High school students visit the Glenn Research Center Manufacturing Facility and learn about careers in the STEM manufacturing field. NASA/Marvin Smith

Call for Peer Reviewers of NAS_Hub Proposals

NASA Research and Education Support Services is seeking persons willing to serve as peer reviewers for the NASA Aerospace Skilled Technical Workforce Hubs (NAS_Hub) opportunity.

Information about this opportunity, proposal requirements and evaluation criteria can be found on the NAS_Hub landing page in the NASA Solicitation and Proposal Integrated Review and Evaluation System (NSPIRES).

Peer review is a critical component of the decision-making process for awarding projects. By engaging individuals with different backgrounds, perspectives, and areas of expertise in the review process, your participation will assist in identifying high-quality projects.

The peer review process is completed in two stages. The proposals are first reviewed individually and anonymously online. When the online review period ends, a panel will convene to discuss the proposals and identify those recommended for funding.

The Online Review Period is scheduled for March 30-April 10, 2026. Proposal narratives are 15 pages and every effort will be made to assign no more than 8 proposals. Non-civil servant online reviewers will receive $75 for each proposal review completed and submitted in NSPIRES prior to the close of the review period.

Note: If selected as an online reviewer, a mandatory training will be held on March 30, 2026.

The Virtual Panel Review is scheduled to take place between the dates of April 20-29, 2026. Specific days have not yet been selected. Non-civil servant panelists will receive a $200/per day honorarium.

Note: If selected as a Virtual Panelist, a mandatory training will be held on April 16, 2026.

Willingness to serve as a peer review does not guarantee selection. Reviewer selection is based on the subject matter of the proposals received. If selected, you will be notified via email, with detailed instructions on the process.

If you are interested in participating in the peer review process, please click here to complete the NAS_Hub Reviewer Response Form, no later than March 12, 2026

Inquiries regarding this request should be submitted via email to NAS_Hub@nasaprs.com

FY26 NASA Aerospace Skilled Technical Workforce Hubs (NAS_Hub)

This opportunity seeks proposals to establish state or regionally focused Skilled Technical Workforce Hubs (NAS_Hubs) that will serve as strategic centers for developing and sustaining a skilled technical workforce aligned with aerospace industry and NASA mission needs. The NAS_Hub Notice of Funding Opportunity has been released and may be found in NASA’s Solicitation and Proposal Integrated Review and Evaluation System (NSPIRES) on the NAS_Hub landing page.

Key Dates

Pre-Proposal Webinar: February 18, 2026, 2:00 to 3:00 p.m. ET

Office Hours Session: March 3, 2026, 2:00 to 3:00 p.m. ET

Proposal Deadline: March 23, 2026, at 11:59 p.m. ET

Anticipated Award Notification: June 2026

About the Opportunity

The rapid expansion of the space economy and renewed national priorities in human space exploration have created an urgent demand for a robust skilled technical workforce—individuals in critical science- and engineering-based roles who do not require a bachelor’s degree. To address nationwide shortages and ensure U.S. competitiveness in aerospace and defense, NASA’s Office of STEM Engagement is launching the NASA Aerospace Skilled Technical Workforce Hubs (NAS_Hub) initiative.

Through this notice NASA seeks lead organizations to establish hubs that:

Collaborate with aerospace employers to align education and training with industry-defined workforce needs.

Partner with community colleges and high school Career and Technical Education programs to deliver hands-on, industry-aligned learning experiences.

Coordinate with state or regional workforce development system.

Build clear and sustainable employment pathways into high-demand aerospace technical careers.

NAS_Hubs will serve as focal points for aligning education, workforce, industry, and government partners to accelerate workforce readiness over a three-year period of performance.

Eligibility

Eligible applicants include:

State, county, city, township, special district, and tribal governments

Public and private institutions of higher education

Nonprofit organizations

For-profit organizations and small businesses

Proposals must include partnerships with, at a minimum:

Three aerospace industry collaborators supporting NASA’s work

Community college career and technical education program

High school career and technical education program

State or regional workforce development system

NASA Center or other NASA facility

Award Information

Maximum Annual Award : $500,000

$500,000 Maximum Total Award: $1,500,000 over three years

Cost Sharing: Not required

How to Apply

Proposals must be submitted electronically via NSPIRES. Registration in NSPIRES and an active SAM.gov registration are required.

Pre-Proposal Webinar and Technical Assistance

NASA will host an interactive pre-proposal webinar for the NAS_Hub opportunity on Feb. 18, 2026, from 2:00 to 3:00 p.m. ET. This session will provide an in-depth overview of this funding opportunity, including program goals, eligibility requirements, proposal preparation guidance, and submission tips. Proposers will also have the opportunity to receive technical assistance and clarification from NASA staff. Prior to attending a webinar, proposers are strongly encouraged to review the full NAS_Hub notice and to check the NAS_Hub landing page in NSPIRES regularly for updates and additional guidance.

Please note that registration is required for the webinar. Connection details will be provided upon completion of registration. Webinar dates, times, registration links, and connection information will be posted on the NAS_Hub landing page in NSPIRES, which also will feature recordings and presentation materials from the webinar after the event for those unable to attend.

Office Hours Session

March 3, 2026, 2:00 to 3:00 p.m. ET

Last opportunity for questions prior to the proposal deadline. Join the office hours session here.

Contact Information

For technical assistance with NSPIRES

NSPIRES Help Desk available Monday–Friday, 8:00 AM–6:00 PM ET

(202) 479-9376

nspires-help@nasaprs.com

Program Contact

Maria Arredondo

Next Gen STEM Program Manager

NASA Office of STEM Engagement

Mary W. Jackson NASA Headquarters

Email: NAS_Hub@nasaprs.com

The Project F.I.R.E. team receives their “Future Game-Changer” award during the 2024 Gateways to Blue Skies forum held at NASA’s Ames Research Center in California. Gateways to Blue Skies is one of several Aeronautics Innovation Challenges open to the academic community. NASA / Brandon Torres

Aeronautics Innovation Challenges – OPEN

NASA’s nationwide team of aeronautical innovators are committed to giving students of all ages opportunities to solve some of the biggest technical challenges facing the aviation community today. Through NASA-sponsored challenges and competitions, students representing multiple disciplines will put their skills to work by designing and building solutions to real-world problems.

Currently Closed Solicitations

Advanced Capabilities for Emergency Response Operations RFI – CLOSED

View the full ACERO RFI announcement here.

NASA’s Advanced Capabilities for Emergency Response Operations (ACERO) project used this request for information to identify technologies that addressed current challenges facing the wildland firefighting community. NASA was seeking information on data collection, airborne connectivity and communications solutions, unmanned aircraft systems traffic management, aircraft operations and autonomy, and more. This would support development of a partnership strategy for future collaborative demonstrations.

Interested parties were requested to respond to this notice with an information package submitted via https://nari.arc.nasa.gov/acero-rfi no later than 4 pm ET, October 15, 2023. Submissions were accepted only from U.S. companies.

Advanced Air Mobility Mission RFI – CLOSED

View the full AAM RFI announcement here.

This request for information is being used to gather market research for NASA to make informed decisions regarding potential partnership strategies and future research to enable Advanced Air Mobility (AAM). NASA is seeking information from public, private, and academic organizations to determine technical needs and community interests that may lead to future solicitations regarding AAM research and development.

This particular RFI is just one avenue of multiple planned opportunities for formal feedback on or participation in NASA’s AAM Mission-related efforts to develop these requirements and help enable AAM.

The respond by date for this RFI closed on Feb. 1, 2025, at 6 p.m. EST.

ROA-2025 NRA Amendment 1 – CLOSED

Advanced Air Vehicles Program Fellowship Opportunities

(View the full ROA-2025 NRA Amendment 1 text here.)

This announcement solicits proposals from accredited U.S. institutions for research training grants to begin the academic year. This Notice of Funding Opportunity is designed to support independently conceived research projects by highly qualified graduate students in disciplines needed to help advance NASA’s mission, thus affording these students the opportunity to directly contribute to advancements in STEM-related areas of study. These opportunities are focused on innovation and the generation of measurable research results that contribute to NASA’s current and future science and technology goals.

Research proposals are sought to address the key challenges summarized in the Elements section at the end of the Amendment 1 document, and which reference NASA’s Hypersonic Technology project.

Reflecting the Fiscal Year 2026 budget changes, the Transformational Tools & Technologies project opportunities originally described in this announcement were cancelled. Proposals citing this project will not be evaluated.

Notices of Intent are not required.

A budget breakdown for each proposal is required, detailing the allocation of the award funds by year. The budget document may adhere to any format or template provided by the applicant’s institution. Two pre-proposal teleconferences for potential proposers will be held and meeting links will be posted on NSPIRES.

Proposals were due by 5 p.m. EDT on June 11, 2025.

NASA Research Opportunities in Aeronautics

Competition for NRA awards is open to both academia and industry.

The current open solicitations for ARMD Research Opportunities are ROA-2024 and ROA-2025.

Here is some general information to know about the NRA process.
articles.reasoning: The article describes NASA's Aeronautics Research Mission Directorate's open solicitations and initiatives to develop a skilled aerospace technical workforce and advance aeronautical innovation. This has broad significance for workforce development, STEM education, and aerospace technology advancement, benefiting society by addressing critical industry needs and fostering innovation. The detailed description of funding opportunities, partnerships, and educational outreach provides substantial context and substance.
articles.personality_title: NASA invites proposals to build skilled aerospace workforce and boost aeronautics research
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA’s Aeronautics Research Mission Directorate (ARMD) supports new research and innovation to improve air transportation systems. To do this, NASA offers opportunities for people and organizations to work with them on projects that develop aerospace technology and train skilled workers.

**What happened** – NASA recently updated its list of open calls for research proposals and partnerships. One key opportunity is the NASA Aerospace Skilled Technical Workforce Hubs (NAS_Hub) initiative. This program aims to create centers that train workers for aerospace jobs, focusing on people who don’t need a college degree but have important technical skills. NASA is asking organizations to submit proposals by March 23, 2026, to establish these hubs. There is also a call for experts to review these proposals. In addition, NASA offers contests for students to solve real aviation challenges and supports research training grants for graduate students.

**Impact** – This initiative is important because the aerospace industry needs more trained workers who have hands-on skills. By creating local hubs that connect schools, employers, and workforce programs, NASA hopes to prepare more people for good jobs in aerospace and keep the United States competitive. The program also encourages partnerships across government, education, and industry, making training more relevant and effective.

**What's next step** – Organizations interested in creating a NAS_Hub can attend a webinar on February 18, 2026, and an office hours session on March 3, 2026, to learn how to apply. After the March 23 deadline, NASA will review proposals and announce awards by June 2026. Selected hubs will work over three years to build training programs and job pathways.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA is inviting proposals to build regional training centers that prepare skilled workers for aerospace careers, supporting innovation and workforce growth in the aviation field.
articles.personality_title_fr: La NASA lance un appel à propositions pour former une main-d'œuvre aérospatiale qualifiée et stimuler la recherche aéronautique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La Direction de la mission de recherche en aéronautique (ARMD) de la NASA soutient de nouvelles recherches et innovations pour améliorer les systèmes de transport aérien. Pour cela, la NASA offre des opportunités aux personnes et organisations de collaborer sur des projets de développement technologique et de formation.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – La NASA a récemment mis à jour sa liste d’appels à propositions ouverts. Une opportunité clé est l’initiative des Centres de main-d’œuvre technique qualifiée en aérospatiale (NAS_Hub). Ce programme vise à créer des centres formant des travailleurs pour des emplois aérospatiaux, ciblant ceux sans diplôme universitaire mais ayant des compétences techniques importantes. La date limite pour soumettre une proposition est le 23 mars 2026. La NASA recherche aussi des experts pour évaluer ces propositions. De plus, la NASA organise des concours pour les étudiants et soutient des bourses de recherche pour les diplômés.

**Impact** – Cette initiative est importante car l’industrie aérospatiale a besoin de plus de travailleurs formés avec des compétences pratiques. En créant des centres locaux qui relient écoles, employeurs et programmes de formation, la NASA espère préparer davantage de personnes à des emplois qualifiés et maintenir la compétitivité des États-Unis. Le programme encourage aussi les partenariats entre gouvernement, éducation et industrie, rendant la formation plus adaptée.

**Prochaine étape** – Les organisations intéressées peuvent assister à un webinaire le 18 février 2026 et à une session de questions le 3 mars 2026 pour apprendre à postuler. Après la date limite du 23 mars, la NASA examinera les propositions et annoncera les résultats en juin 2026. Les centres sélectionnés travailleront sur trois ans pour développer des programmes de formation et des parcours professionnels.

**En résumé** – La NASA invite à créer des centres régionaux de formation pour préparer des travailleurs qualifiés aux carrières aérospatiales, soutenant l’innovation et la croissance de la main-d’œuvre dans le domaine de l’aviation.
articles.personality_title_es: NASA invita a presentar propuestas para formar mano de obra aeroespacial capacitada y potenciar la investigación aeronáutica
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La Dirección de Misión de Investigación Aeronáutica (ARMD) de la NASA apoya nuevas investigaciones e innovaciones para mejorar los sistemas de transporte aéreo. Para ello, la NASA ofrece oportunidades para que personas y organizaciones colaboren en proyectos que desarrollen tecnología aeroespacial y formen trabajadores capacitados.

**Qué sucedió** – La NASA actualizó recientemente su lista de convocatorias abiertas para propuestas y asociaciones. Una oportunidad clave es la iniciativa Centros de Mano de Obra Técnica Capacitada en Aeroespacial (NAS_Hub). Este programa busca crear centros que formen trabajadores para empleos aeroespaciales, enfocándose en personas que no necesitan título universitario pero tienen habilidades técnicas importantes. La fecha límite para enviar propuestas es el 23 de marzo de 2026. También se busca expertos para revisar estas propuestas. Además, la NASA ofrece concursos para estudiantes y becas de investigación para estudiantes de posgrado.

**Impacto** – Esta iniciativa es importante porque la industria aeroespacial necesita más trabajadores capacitados con habilidades prácticas. Al crear centros locales que conecten escuelas, empleadores y programas de formación, la NASA espera preparar a más personas para buenos empleos y mantener la competitividad de EE.UU. El programa también fomenta asociaciones entre gobierno, educación e industria, haciendo la formación más relevante.

**Próximo paso** – Las organizaciones interesadas pueden asistir a un seminario web el 18 de febrero de 2026 y a una sesión de preguntas el 3 de marzo de 2026 para aprender a postular. Después del plazo del 23 de marzo, la NASA revisará las propuestas y anunciará los resultados en junio de 2026. Los centros seleccionados trabajarán durante tres años para desarrollar programas de formación y rutas laborales.

**Resumen en una frase** – La NASA invita a crear centros regionales para formar trabajadores capacitados en carreras aeroespaciales, apoyando la innovación y el crecimiento laboral en la aviación.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of interconnected mechanical gears shaped like aircraft wings and technical tools, gently glowing with soft natural tones, set against a calm sky background symbolizing collaboration and growth in aerospace education and workforce development.
